Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendants To Compel Responses To Written Discovery; To Compel Attendance At Deposition; And To Deem Admitted Requestes For Admisson; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
Off Calendar Jan-29-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance.

DEFENDANTS REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, PAUL STEWART, M.D.'S Motion Of Defendants To Compel Responses To Written Discovery; To Compel Attendance At Deposition; And To Deem Admitted Requestes For Admisson. The Court's tentative ruling is vacated. Motion is ordered off calendar by Judge Pro Tem James Fleming pursuant to Erin C. Pine's email of January 28, 2020. Judge Pro Tem: James Fleming; Clerk: William Trupek; Not reported. (302/JPT)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-12-2019 Set For Trial On May-04-20 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-07-2019 Continued To Sep-25-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-03-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Edward Freidberg, esq. (916-929-9060) for Plaintiff via Court Call.
David A. Depolo, esq. (925-287-8181) of Donnelly Nelson Depolo Murray & Efremsky for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tenative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANTs REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA and PAUL STEWART, M.D.'s DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Defendants Regents of University of California and Paul Stewart, M.D.'s demurrer to Plaintiffs Kaden Woodall, Conrad Woodall, and Rebecca Woodall's complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to the second and fourth causes of action for fraud. "[E]ven though the plaintiff alleges false representations on the part of the physician or fraudulent concealment, our courts have always treated the action as one for malpractice." (Tell v. Taylor (1961) 191 Cal.App.2d 266, 271.) Based on the conduct alleged in the complaint, this claim is more properly stated as one for medical malpractice because the harm stems from Dr. Stewart's diagnosis and expression of medical opinion. (Compl. 45-48; see Larson v. UHS of Rancho Springs, Inc. (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 336, 347 ["when a plaintiff asserts a claim against a health care provider on a legal theory other than professional negligence, courts must determine whether the claim is nonetheless based on the health care provider's professional negligence" by examining the legal theory alleged and the provider's alleged conduct] [emphasis in original].) Further, Plaintiffs fail to allege facts to show that Dr. Stewart intended to induce Plaintiffs' reliance on his representations about the cancer in Kaden Woodall's eye. Because Plaintiffs have already pled causes of action for medical malpractice, there would be no reason to allow leave to amend.

Hart v. Browne (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 947 is distinguishable because that case involved a physician who gave allegedly fraudulent advice to attorneys about another doctor's liability for medical malpractice. (Id. at 951.) It could be inferred from medical records and the questions asked by the attorneys that the doctor's statements were false and likely to be relied upon. (Id. at 958.)

Counsel for Defendant to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR#6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
